contraindications.clinicalRationale: ACE inhibitors cause teratogenesis with major congenital malformations, predominantly cardiovascular and neurologic abnormalities, when exposed in first trimester. Risk of fetal and neonatal complications throughout pregnancy.
contraindications.riskDescription: Major congenital malformations, predominantly cardiovascular and neurologic abnormalities
contraindications.sourceSnippet: born between 1985 and 2000, which demonstrated that the risks of major congenital malformations, predominantly cardiovascular and neurologic abnormalities, were significantly increased among infants exposed to an ACEi in the first trimester

contraindications.clinicalRationale: ACE inhibitors can cause hypotension. Symptomatic hypotension requires dose reduction or discontinuation to prevent hemodynamic compromise.
contraindications.riskDescription: Hemodynamic compromise from excessive blood pressure reduction
contraindications.alternativeConsiderations: Discontinue other concurrent blood pressure medications before reducing ACE inhibitor dose
contraindications.sourceSnippet: Reduce the dose or discontinue ACEi or ARB therapy in the setting of either symptomatic hypotension or uncontrolled hyperkalemia despite the medical treatment outlined in Practice Point 1.2.5

contraindications.clinicalRationale: ACE inhibitors block the renin-angiotensin system leading to hyperkalemia. Uncontrolled hyperkalemia despite medical management poses risk of cardiac arrhythmias.
contraindications.riskDescription: Potentially life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias from hyperkalemia
contraindications.alternativeConsiderations: Medical management with diuretics, sodium bicarbonate, or potassium binders before dose reduction
contraindications.sourceSnippet: Reduce the dose or discontinue ACEi or ARB therapy in the setting of either symptomatic hypotension or uncontrolled hyperkalemia despite the medical treatment outlined in PP 1.2.5

conditionDescriptions: End-stage renal disease with uremic symptoms
contraindications.clinicalRationale: In advanced CKD with eGFR <15 ml/min per 1.73 m2, ACE inhibitors may need discontinuation to reduce uremic symptoms and allow time for kidney replacement therapy preparation.
contraindications.riskDescription: Worsening uremic symptoms and delayed preparation for kidney replacement therapy
contraindications.alternativeConsiderations: Temporary discontinuation with aim to restart after kidney replacement therapy
contraindications.sourceSnippet: Reduce the dose or discontinue ACEi or ARB therapy in the setting of either symptomatic hypotension or uncontrolled hyperkalemia despite the medical treatment outlined in Practice Point 1.2.5, or to reduce uremic symptoms while treating kidney failure (estimated glomerular filtration rate [eGFR] <15 ml/min per 1.73 m2)

conditionDescriptions: Acute kidney injury with >30% creatinine rise
contraindications.clinicalRationale: Acute rise in serum creatinine >30% within 4 weeks indicates potential acute kidney injury, volume depletion, or renovascular disease requiring investigation and possible dose adjustment.
contraindications.riskDescription: Progressive acute kidney injury and loss of kidney function
contraindications.alternativeConsiderations: Investigate for renovascular disease, volume depletion, or concurrent nephrotoxic drugs before permanent discontinuation
contraindications.sourceSnippet: Continue ACEi or ARB therapy unless serum creatinine rises by more than 30% within 4 weeks following initiation of treatment or an increase in dose

contraindications.clinicalRationale: Angioedema is a known serious adverse effect of ACE inhibitors with weighted incidence of 0.30%. Can cause life-threatening airway obstruction.
contraindications.alternativeConsiderations: Switch to ARB for patients who develop angioedema on ACE inhibitors
contraindications.sourceSnippet: Angioedema has been associated with the use of ACEi, with a weighted incidence of 0.30%. Dry cough is also a known adverse effect of ACEi, affecting about 10% of patients. Consideration can be given to switching affected patients to an ARB.

warnings.warningText: ACE inhibitor-induced dry cough affects about 10% of patients
warnings.actionRequired: Consider switching to ARB if cough is intolerable while maintaining RAS blockade benefits

warnings.warningText: Monitor serum creatinine and potassium within 2-4 weeks of initiation or dose increase
warnings.actionRequired: Check serum creatinine and potassium levels within 2-4 weeks after starting or changing dose, then annually

warnings.warningText: Advise contraception in women of childbearing age receiving ACE inhibitor therapy
warnings.actionRequired: Counsel regarding contraception needs and pregnancy risks. Discontinue immediately if pregnancy occurs.

warnings.warningText: Close monitoring of serum potassium required when eGFR <30 ml/min per 1.73 m2
warnings.affectedPopulation: Patients with eGFR <30 ml/min per 1.73 m2
warnings.actionRequired: Increase frequency of potassium monitoring in advanced CKD. Consider potassium management strategies.

warnings.warningText: Use only one RAS blocking agent at a time. Combination with ARB or direct renin inhibitor is potentially harmful.
warnings.actionRequired: Avoid dual RAS blockade. Do not combine ACE inhibitor with ARB or direct renin inhibitor.
